one_line_takeaway: A rigorous first large-scale study showing that a small-toolset LLM agent (K-REPRO) can autonomously reproduce >50% (up to 64% with re-runs) of real KernelCTF Linux-kernel N-day bugs at a few dollars and tens of minutes each, beating the non-LLM SOTA SyzDirect in both rate and speed, with race conditions, temporal-memory bugs, and missing commit messages as the main limiters and root-cause analysis (not tooling) as the true bottleneck.
key_finding: LLM agents CAN reproduce a substantial fraction of real-world exploitable Linux kernel vulnerabilities autonomously: XHigh reproduces 56.0% and Medium 47.0% in a single run over 100 KernelCTF bugs, rising to 64% when failed cases are re-run (converging after 3 runs). Success is robust to knowledge cutoff (pre- vs post-cutoff 58.6% vs 52.4% for XHigh, not significant) and to removing tools (GDB removal costs XHigh only 1pp; removing nft/tc utilities does not significantly change success, agents compensate with direct syscalls/library APIs). The two things that DO hurt: race conditions (XHigh 29.2% vs 64.5% non-race) and temporal memory bugs (UAF/DF significantly harder than OOB even after adjusting for race: OR 0.13, p=0.002 Medium; 0.20, p=0.023 XHigh), plus loss of informative commit messages (removing them drops XHigh to 39% and Medium to 36%). Against SyzDirect on the aligned 85-case set, K-REPRO (XHigh) reproduces 67.1% (57/85) vs SyzDirect's 42% (42/100) at ~22 min/case overall vs ~18.85 h/case. Failure analysis of 13 always-failing cases: 8 due to incorrect/incomplete root-cause analysis, 3 environment issues, 2 race-window issues; GDB rarely corrects a wrong hypothesis, it mostly reaffirms plausible ones, so accurate root-cause analysis is the real bottleneck.
core_contribution: The paper presents K-REPRO, a fully automated LLM-based agentic system for reproducing N-day vulnerabilities in the Linux kernel, and uses it to conduct what the authors claim is the first large-scale empirical study of LLM-agent-based kernel vulnerability reproduction. K-REPRO takes only a security patch commit ID as input, deterministically (non-LLM) builds a vulnerable kernel image (one commit prior to the fix) inside a QEMU VM, then runs an autonomous agent that couples an LLM with a small toolset (code browsing, VM management, VM interaction, and GDB debugging) to generate a working proof-of-concept (PoC) without Internet access. The contribution is both the system and the measurement study: characterizing effectiveness/efficiency/stability (RQ1), ablating tools and input signals to find what actually drives performance (RQ2), and comparing against the non-LLM state of the art SyzDirect (RQ3). Note: AIxCC, ReachabilityAgent, and the LLM-for-fuzzing works are cited context/motivation, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: N-day (post-patch) Linux kernel vulnerabilities. Because upstream security patches take dozens of days (sometimes >6 months) to port into stable/LTS and downstream distros (Ubuntu, Android OEM), a window exists in which a patch is public (usable by attackers for analysis) but downstream systems remain unpatched. The task is dual-use: offensively, PoC reproduction is a prerequisite for N-day exploit development against unpatched targets; defensively, it aids vulnerable-version identification and remediation prioritization via exploitability assessment. The paper studies capability rather than proposing a defense; it explicitly weighs both offensive and defensive implications.
limitations: (1) 'Any kernel crash = success' is a coarse proxy; automated root-cause equivalence between crashes is not verified (mitigated only by manual inspection of XHigh successes and by cheating-detection rules). (2) Data-leakage / memorization cannot be fully excluded since many KernelCTF bugs have public writeups before the cutoff; the cutoff analysis and post-cutoff one-shot successes only partially address it. (3) The SyzDirect comparison relies on SyzDirect's self-reported statistics (not a re-run), and on a different, easier dataset composition (Syzbot-discoverable bugs; only 3 of the KernelCTF cases overlap Syzbot), and SyzDirect could not be run on newer kernels at all. (4) KernelCTF skews toward net/netfilter/bpf user-accessible subsystems; generalization to the full kernel attack surface is only partially probed via the Syzbot subset. (5) Results are specific to two GPT-5.1 Codex models and the Codex CLI/MCP harness; other models/frameworks untested. (6) Race conditions and temporal-memory bugs remain weak spots and the paper offers design directions (hypothesis diversification, early falsification) but no implemented fix. (7) No monetary cost cap and a 10-hour budget could inflate reachable success versus stricter real-world limits.

datasets_used: KernelCTF (Google VRP): 100 real-world exploitable Linux kernel vulnerabilities retained from 116 originals (disclosed 2023-2025)
SyzDirect-aligned Syzbot subset: 85 cases (rebuilt from the SyzDirect paper's reported Syzbot case identifiers, from 100 originals) used for the RQ3 comparison
Failure case-study set: 13 KernelCTF cases that never succeeded in any setting and have public technical writeups
quantitative_results: Aggregated over repeated runs: 63/100 succeed in at least one of two XHigh runs; converges to 64% after a third run
Avg cost per case incl. failures: $4.02 (XHigh), $4.66 (Medium); success-only $2.49 / $2.57
Avg time per case: 19.33 min (XHigh), 33.80 min (Medium); success-only 11.89 / 18.67 min
Race vs non-race success: XHigh 29.2% (7/24) vs 64.5% (49/76); Medium 20.8% (5/24) vs 55.3% (42/76)
UAF/DF vs OOB race-adjusted odds ratio (CMH): 0.13 (p=0.002) Medium, 0.20 (p=0.023) XHigh
Removing commit messages drops success to XHigh 39.0%, Medium 36.0%
GDB removal: XHigh -1pp, Medium unchanged; utility (nft/tc) removal XHigh 56.0%->46.0% (OR 0.67, p=0.20), Medium 47.0%->50.0% (n.s.); prompt degradation XHigh 56->50, Medium 47->46 (n.s.)
Knowledge cutoff (XHigh): pre-cutoff 58.6% (34/58) vs post-cutoff 52.4% (22/42), not significant (OR 1.29, p=0.55)
Tool calls/case: XHigh 129.5 (55.5 VM interaction, 40.5 code browse, 24.4 debug, 9.2 VM mgmt), Medium 165.1
PoC iterations: XHigh avg 7.4 (4.9 for successes), Medium 9.1 (4.5); one-shot successes 10.7% XHigh, 21.3% Medium

vs SyzDirect on 85 aligned cases: K-REPRO (XHigh) 57/85 (67.1%) vs SyzDirect 42/100 (42.0%); success-time 17.04 min vs 11.74 h; overall-time 22.48 min vs 18.85 h; K-REPRO avg cost $2.19/case ($1.58 success)
Failure breakdown (13 cases): 8 incorrect/incomplete root-cause analysis, 3 environment issues, 2 unmet race windows
baselines_compared: SyzDirect (state-of-the-art non-LLM directed greybox fuzzing for Linux kernel bug reproduction) - primary quantitative baseline
Internal ablation baselines: full K-REPRO vs degraded prompt, vs no subsystem CLI utilities (nft/tc), vs no GDB, vs no commit messages
GPT-5.1 Codex Max (XHigh) vs GPT-5.1 Codex (Medium) as cross-model comparison
